responses.strategic_rationale: The plan confronts core tensions between maximizing data quality and minimizing participant burden, maintaining safety within a mid-size residential unit, and enabling robust external validation while preserving privacy. The levers focus on (1) optimizing sensing to capture meaningful parasomnia events with minimal intrusion; (2) accelerating and diversifying recruitment without sacrificing cohort representativeness; (3) tightening data governance to enable responsible data sharing; (4) engineering an efficient staffing model that sustains 24/7 coverage within budget; (5) embracing adaptive planning to avoid resource misallocation; (6) building a scalable semi-automated event triage to reduce manual review while preserving scoring rigor. Collectively, they balance speed, data integrity, ethical constraints, and feasibility essential for a rigorous early-stage clinical research facility.

levers.name: Tiered sensing deployment and escalation protocol
levers.consequences: Shifting to a two-tier regime increases data density for routine nights while limiting intrusiveness on most nights. Downstream, fuller PSG on escalation nights improves diagnostic resolution for flagged events but demands more technician time and higher equipment utilization, potentially reducing overall nightly capacity.
options: Implement a two-tier monitoring regime that prioritizes dry-electrode EEG headbands and mattress sensors for the majority of nights, reserving full PSG on escalation nights when flagged events occur.
Adopt a fixed nightly schedule for enhanced PSG to occur every third night across all participants, ensuring sufficient data density while maintaining participant comfort and safety margins.
Introduce randomization of escalation nights across participants to prevent systematic bias and assess the impact of fuller PSG on event capture without inducing Hawthorne effects.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: data yield vs participant burden and scheduling feasibility. Weakness: none of the options explicitly account for day-to-day variability in sleep schedules or hospital operational disruptions that can affect escalation fidelity.

levers.name: Recruitment cadence and control-cohort integration
levers.consequences: Prioritizing recruitment channels aligned to the Bonn ecosystem accelerates enrollment and improves screening consistency. Downstream, faster enrollment may compress time for thorough data quality checks and increase risk of selection bias if eligibility criteria are tightened too aggressively.
options: Coordinate recruitment through the Bonn sleep clinic and regional networks to concentrate on high-probability candidates with clear pre-admission pathways, accelerating enrollment velocity.
Implement a rolling 18-week recruitment window with interim reviews to adjust eligibility criteria and broaden the pool of eligible participants if early screening yields lag.
Incorporate a matched control recruitment stream in parallel and align consent workflows to optimize data quality and comparability across cohorts.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: enrollment velocity vs representativeness. Weakness: options lack explicit safeguards against selection bias or demographic imbalances.

levers.name: Data governance and privacy framework
levers.consequences: Strengthening governance clarifies data access and sharing boundaries, enabling external validation while preserving privacy. Downstream, stricter controls may slow collaborations and extend timelines for data-use agreements, potentially delaying publications.
options: Institute a staged data access protocol that grants researchers tiered read permissions, ensuring de-identified sensor data is shareable while preserving video privacy.
Deploy automated redaction and consent-tracking systems to maintain compliance with privacy restrictions, enabling smoother external collaborations within approved data-use agreements.
Pursue a data-sharing framework that deposits de-identified physiological data into recognized repositories, while keeping video and identifying metadata locally under governance review.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: enabling external validation while preserving privacy. Weakness: governance timelines and ethics approvals are omitted.

levers.name: Staffing model and shift design
levers.consequences: Reconfiguring staffing improves 24/7 observation coverage and safety margins while balancing operational risk. Downstream, higher staffing flexibility can increase personnel costs and require more robust fatigue management and turnover controls.
options: Reorganize staffing into overlapping eight-hour shifts with on-call coverage to ensure 24/7 observation capability while maintaining clinician safety margins.
Cross-train technicians to perform both scoring and device maintenance, reducing handoffs and improving data throughput during high-activity weeks.
Introduce a floating backfill role to maintain continuity during turnover while enforcing strict onboarding and ongoing ethics training to safeguard data integrity.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: safe staffing vs cost. Weakness: no explicit minimum clinician-to-participant ratios or fatigue thresholds.

levers.name: Adaptive sample sizing and interim evaluation
levers.consequences: Instituting interim analyses helps avoid misalignment between enrollment pace and data yield, enabling resource reallocation as needed. Downstream, adaptive decisions may introduce governance complexity and potential delays if pre-specified criteria are not clear.
options: Establish predefined interim analyses at 6 and 12 months to recalibrate target enrollment and event-capture thresholds using observed event yields, guiding resource allocation.
Implement a protocol-triggered recruitment-review process at 18 months if events per participant fall below a threshold, enabling scope adjustment or criteria refinement.
Develop a decision framework that uses interim data to realign aims toward phenotyping depth rather than recruitment quantity, preserving study power.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing depth of phenotyping with event yield. Weakness: lacks explicit stopping rules or pre-registered adaptive criteria.

levers.name: Semi-automated event triage development and validation
levers.consequences: Developing a semi-automated triage aims to rank probable parasomnia episodes, reducing manual review workload while maintaining scoring accuracy. Downstream, performance depends on robust validation and consistent inter-rater reliability, with risks if models overfit to initial data.
options: Prototype and validate a semi-automated event-triage workspace that ranks likely parasomnia episodes, reducing manual review time while maintaining dual-scoring accuracy.
Pilot a cross-lab benchmarking study with an external partner to assess generalizability of the triage model before full deployment.
Invest in an auditable annotation workflow with synchronized timing metadata and structured scoring templates to improve inter-rater reliability and data traceability.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: reliability of semi-automated scoring vs manual gold standard. Weakness: insufficient plan for handling conflicting automated scores or edge cases.

responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn residential parasomnia unit operates at the intersection of participant safety, data integrity, and sustainable funding. Core tensions center on enforcing robust privacy and safety guardrails without impeding timely data capture, and on scaling recruitment, annotation throughput, and capacity within a fixed budget. The six levers target governance clarity, scalable enrollment, data Quality processes, modular sensing interoperability, prudent expansion triggers, and safety-conscious operations to balance research productivity with ethical and regulatory obligations. Together, they enable iterative learning across phases of the pilot while preserving the domestic, naturalistic environment essential to capturing genuine behavior.

levers.name: Residential safety and privacy guardrails expansion
levers.consequences: Enhancing residential safety and privacy guardrails reduces the risk of incidents and unauthorized data exposure during long stays. It also increases operational overhead for audits, training, and access controls, potentially slowing data access but improving participant trust and regulatory compliance.
options: Implement tiered privacy-preserving data access with role-based permissions, local de-identification, and audit trails to minimize participant risk while preserving research value.
Add automated safety lapse detection and escalation triggers tied to nurse-driven incident response protocols to reduce risk during high-sensitivity nights.
Institute independent data access reviews and privacy impact assessments on a quarterly basis to align with evolving regulatory expectations and maintain public trust.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: strengthening safety and privacy can slow data access and analysis. Weakness: the options do not quantify the incremental staffing or training burden, or the impact on recruitment velocity.

levers.name: Scaled recruitment funnel and onboarding milestones
levers.consequences: Scaled recruitment funnel and onboarding milestones create higher-quality cohorts and reduce early dropout, improving downstream data integrity and study power. The approach introduces additional administrative steps and longer onboarding timelines, which may constrain speed to first data capture.
options: Institute staged consent and onboarding milestones tied to pre-screen data quality checks to improve enrollment yield and early data integrity.
Pilot regional outreach partnerships with local clinicians to diversify referrals and reduce time-to-enrollment bottlenecks.
Deploy flexible compensation adjustments balanced with budget discipline and regulatory compliance to sustain participation without inflating costs.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: reliance on external partnerships may introduce coordination delays; Weakness: the options overlook explicit metrics for referral quality and regulatory alignment.

levers.name: Adaptive annotation throughput and quality assurance regime
levers.consequences: Adaptive annotation throughput and quality assurance regime improves data labeling speed and reliability, enabling more timely analyses. It also requires sustained training and monitoring, which may divert time from recruitment or facility maintenance.
options: Institute continuous training cycles for annotators with blinded dual scoring and consensus reviews to improve inter-rater reliability.
Integrate a rotating annotation audit desk with weekly feedback loops to catch drift and maintain scoring consistency.
Pilot cross-site annotation tasks with external experts to diversify perspectives and accelerate labeling for rarer event types.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: human-centered scoring is a bottleneck requiring significant time; Weakness: none of the options address automation or active learning to reduce manual workload.

levers.name: Modular sensing architecture and data interoperability standardization
levers.consequences: Modular sensing architecture and data interoperability standardization reduces vendor lock-in and accelerates upgrades, enabling protocol iterations with less integration risk. It also requires upfront design work and validation to ensure interoperability across devices and data integrity.
options: Standardize sensor interfaces and data schemas to enable plug-and-play upgrades and interchangeability across equipment vendors.
Adopt an EEG-BIDS compatible data pipeline with automated QC checks to minimize manual processing time.
Establish a vendor-agnostic telemetry layer that supports rapid decommissioning and reconfiguration without protocol disruption.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: standardization can constrain device-specific performance and delay procurement; Weakness: options do not lay out a plan for ongoing calibration costs or vendor risk management.

levers.name: Pilot-driven capacity planning and staged expansion trigger
levers.consequences: Pilot-driven capacity planning and staged expansion trigger aligns infrastructure growth with demonstrated outcomes, reducing overbuild risk and budget waste. It also introduces potential delays if thresholds are not met and requires robust forecasting and monitoring.
options: Define explicit KPIs and a go/no-go calendar to expand from 8 to 12 suites only after data quality benchmarks are met.
Create a probabilistic capacity model incorporating variable stay durations to forecast occupancy and staffing needs.
Pre-negotiate lease extensions and conversion options with landlords to preserve flexibility if program success warrants acceleration.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: expansion hinges on performance milestones, which may conflict with urgent research needs; Weakness: does not address budget reallocation governance or contingency plans if funding decreases.

levers.name: Strategic governance and stakeholder alignment framework
levers.consequences: Strategic governance and stakeholder alignment framework clarifies decision rights and milestone expectations, reducing drift and funding risk. It also adds governance overhead and can slow urgent reallocations in response to safety concerns or data findings.
options: Formalize quarterly progress reviews with external advisory board milestones to align funding, ethics, and publication objectives.
Draft formal escalation paths for budget variances and staffing shortages to maintain project continuity.
Publish a transparent data sharing and authorship policy to manage expectations with industry partners and sponsors.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: governance layers risk slowing critical decisions when urgent actions are needed; Weakness: options do not specify mechanisms for exception handling or rapid reallocation during emergencies.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project navigates core tensions between safety and data quality, participant privacy, staffing capacity, and timelines within a residential sleep research setting. The six levers below are selected to build operational resilience, broaden data richness without compromising ethics, sharpen governance without derailing progress, optimize participant yield while preserving safety, and advance semi-automated tools with appropriate human oversight. Collectively, they aim to sustain rigorous phenotyping, enable scalable methods validation, and position the program for a robust phase-two proposal, while guarding against common implementation bottlenecks identified in the plan.

levers.name: Operational risk hedging through flexible staffing and cross-training
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever increases resilience to staffing gaps and supports scalable expansion as data quality permits. It may entail higher training costs and more complex scheduling, which could delay full implementation if staffing pools are slow to onboard.
options: Establish cross-trained night shift teams with rotating roles to sustain safe monitoring coverage across eight suites while maintaining scalable capacity for phased expansion.
Contract a preferred staffing pool and implement a tiered overtime policy to balance coverage during peaks without compromising patient safety or staff well-being.
Introduce formal handoff protocols and fatigue-aware shift scheduling to reduce miscommunication during high-load nights while awaiting longer-term automation.
levers.review_lever: Core tension centers on scaling safety-critical coverage while keeping labor costs and onboarding time in check; a weakness is that none of the options explicitly price risk of extended onboarding or vendor reliability.

levers.name: Expanded data capture scope versus privacy risk
levers.consequences: Expanding data capture improves event characterization and model development but increases privacy risk and data governance burden. It may require additional storage and processing capacity, and could necessitate revised consent and ethics approvals.
options: Pilot enhanced nights with full PSG on a randomized subset to validate event triage while preserving privacy constraints.
Scale sensor suite to include environmental and ambient data across all rooms to improve phenotyping.
Limit data scope to de-identified datasets with strict access controls and data-use agreements to minimize privacy exposure.
levers.review_lever: Core tension is between data richness and privacy governance; weakness: none of the options detail how consent mechanisms or participant opt-out rights will be updated for expanded data capture.

levers.name: Clinical governance integration with external scientific advisory board
levers.consequences: Bringing external oversight can improve transparency and scientific rigor but may slow decision cycles if advisor availability is inconsistent. It also requires clear scope and budgeting to avoid drift from core aims.
options: Formalize quarterly governance reviews that explicitly tie project milestones to ethical compliance updates and funding progress.
Engage external clinicians for blinded second opinions on adjudication to improve reliability at the cost of timeline.
Create a rotating governance liaison role embedded in PI's team to accelerate decision-making with stakeholder input.
levers.review_lever: External oversight enhances quality but can impede momentum; weakness: options do not specify contingency paths if external reviews miss milestones.

levers.name: Participant selection criteria refinement to optimize event yield
levers.consequences: Tightening inclusion criteria can raise average event yield per enrollee but may reduce eligible population and slow enrollment. Conversely, relaxing criteria can broaden recruitment but may introduce heterogeneity that complicates adjudication.
options: Adopt stricter inclusion criteria to target higher event frequency while ensuring representativeness remains acceptable overall.
Relax exclusion criteria for minor comorbidities to increase eligible pool without compromising safety significantly overall.
Implement adaptive recruitment quotas aligned with interim event yield and safety review to optimize throughput.
levers.review_lever: Core tension involves representativeness versus event yield; weakness: options don't address potential recruitment channel biases that could affect sample diversity.

levers.name: Residential environment design balancing naturalistic behavior and safety constraints
levers.consequences: Design decisions affect how naturally participants behave and how complete the data are; balancing ambience with safety can improve data fidelity but may increase construction and maintenance costs. Privacy considerations also shape what can be observed and reviewed.
options: Invest in controlled acoustic and lighting tweaks to simulate home-like ambience without increasing risk during episodes.
Implement modular room dividers and visual cues to reduce wandering while maintaining domestic feel and comfort.
Introduce anonymized video review protocols with privacy-centric access to ensure safety without public exposure online.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: environment realism versus safety constraints; weakness: options do not fully address staff training needs for enforcing new room configurations during events.

levers.name: Semi-automated event triage tool development governance
levers.consequences: This lever can reduce manual review burden and speed up triage but risks over-reliance on imperfect automation if thresholds are not carefully tuned. It also demands robust privacy and data governance for external benchmarking.
options: Pilot a dual-human scoring benchmark with a separate blinded adjudication layer to validate triage outputs.
Open a small, strictly de-identified data subset for external algorithm benchmarking under data-use agreements safeguarding privacy.
Institute a staged rollout with continuous feedback loops from technicians to iterate scoring rules safely.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: automation balance with human oversight; weakness: options miss contingency plans if adjudication backlog occurs or if performance plateaus.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but a regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously — correlation risk absent from every option."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
